text: This exercise is anterior needle drive vertical.
The visual field shows a simulation of suturing in an upwards direction, resembling the anterior needle drive horizontal technique on the anterior abdominal wall.
Two rows of parallel dots are visible, intended for suturing in a top-to-bottom fashion to simulate the closure of a vertical defect.
The surgeon demonstrates suture throws in a lateral to medial orientation, aligning with the parallel rows of dots simulating a vertical defect closure.
The needle enters the lateral dot and exits through the medial dot, demonstrating the suturing technique from lateral to medial orientation on the simulated vertical defect.
The surgeon prepares for a right-sided suture throw by loading the needle in a position that allows for hand pronation during the throw.
The surgeon pulls the needle out following its curve, minimizing excess force or stress on the tissue, while the left hand assists in reloading the needle.
The surgeon performs suturing on the left side, following a top-to-bottom and lateral-to-medial orientation, similar to the previous side.
The surgeon loads the needle into the left hand, preparing to pronate the hand for a successful suture throw.
The surgeon uses the right hand to pull the needle through, following the curve of the needle, to achieve better range of motion and minimize excessive force during the suture throw.
It also increases the efficiency of your reload, as instead of having to pass it back to your opposite hand to reload it properly, you can do it in one single move.
Staying efficient with your movements and moving quickly will allow you to achieve a high score.
However, the exercise is very critical of any excessive force.
So that really has to be taken into account in order to try and achieve the highest score.
